title: INFORM Act
plain_summary: The INFORM Act is a bill that wants to help people in China get real news and information that their government tries to block. The Chinese government controls the internet and stops people from seeing many websites and news stories. This bill would give the US government 75 million dollars each year to create tools and websites that can get around China's internet blocks. It would also create a special news service that shares uncensored news about China in the Chinese language. The goal is to make sure Chinese people can access true information even when their government tries to stop them.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Foreign Relations.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Titles; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Informing a Nation with Free, Open, and Reliable Media Act of two thousand twenty five or the INFORM Act. The law includes nine main sections that cover definitions, findings about internet censorship in China, policy statements, strategies for getting independent information to Chinese citizens, creating a new Global News Service, increasing coordination between government agencies, and addressing unfair treatment between the United States and China in sharing information.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: In this law, several terms have specific meanings. CCP means the Chinese Communist Party. Coordinator means the person the President picks to lead a special group of agencies working together. Department means the Department of State. PRC means the People's Republic of China. RFA means Radio Free Asia. USAGM means the United States Agency for Global Media. VOA means Voice of America.

sections.title: Section 3  Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned several important facts about internet control in China. Since the internet became popular, the Chinese Communist Party has seen the open internet as a threat to its power and control over its citizens. The Party has spent billions of dollars to build a digital control system called the Great Firewall of China that blocks foreign websites, news sites, and communication tools. This system also watches what people do online and punishes them in real life, which makes people censor themselves. Under Chairman Xi Jinping, the Party has focused on censoring and watching citizens online while spreading anti-American messages. The internet censorship in China makes nationalist voices louder while silencing people who disagree. It also hides information that might make the Party look bad, like reports about corruption. While China restricts American online activities in China, it uses America's open internet to spread propaganda against the United States. Despite heavy censorship and constant government messaging, Chinese citizens are starting to show they are not satisfied with their government. They are looking for other sources of information and calling for more freedom. Studies show that when Chinese students see foreign news, it changes what they know and believe. When the Clubhouse app was briefly available in China in two thousand twenty one, it became very popular as people discussed sensitive topics like camps in Xinjiang and the Tiananmen Square massacre of nineteen eighty nine. During the White Paper protests in two thousand twenty two against COVID nineteen lockdowns, people used online platforms to organize. Reports show that VPN use in China doubled in two thousand twenty three, even though VPNs are mostly blocked and illegal. In February two thousand twenty four, when the US Embassy posted about tracking giraffes, Chinese social media users flooded the comments complaining about China's economy and asking America for help. Chinese citizens want independent information sources, but US Government funding to help them get around internet blocks has stayed low compared to the billions China spends on censorship. Publicly funded VPNs are used by millions in China each month, but traditional tools like VPNs are not enough to handle China's complex information control system. Giving Chinese citizens access to independent information will help the United States engage with them, provide reliable news about events in China and around the world, help Americans understand China better, and support Chinese citizens who want more freedom and rights.

sections.title: Section 4  Sense of Congress
sections.text: Congress believes the United States Government should focus on creating a plan for engaging with Chinese citizens by developing and delivering content in Mandarin Chinese that is timely, interesting, and relevant to their daily lives, their government's policies, and the failures and corruption of the Chinese Communist Party. The government should increase coordination between agencies to create and share compelling Chinese language content that is otherwise blocked by China's censored internet. The government should focus both on giving Chinese citizens access to independent information through tools that get around censorship and on secure ways to share that content in China's heavily censored internet system. The government should make these tools work better by pairing them more effectively with timely and compelling Chinese language content. The government should also work to counter the unfair situation where China can operate freely in America's open internet while America faces heavy restrictions in China.

sections.title: Section 5  Statement of Policy
sections.text: The United States has four main policy goals. First, making it easier for the roughly one billion four hundred million Chinese citizens to access independent information and engage with the outside world is a national security priority that helps promote human rights, rule of law, and good government in China and around the world. Second, the United States will prioritize expanding and improving how it develops and shares independent information with Chinese citizens both inside and outside China, including by better pairing independent information with the tools needed to access it. Third, the United States will prioritize and coordinate the creation and sharing of Chinese language content while developing technical solutions to get around China's digital information controls. Fourth, the United States will work with like minded partners and allies to develop coordinated strategies for increasing Chinese citizens' access to independent information and to address the unfair treatment in information and media environments between China and the United States and its partners.

sections.title: Section 6  Strategy for Increasing Access to Independent Information for Citizens of the People's Republic of China
sections.text: Within one year after this law takes effect, the President must submit a strategy to the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations and the House Committee on Foreign Affairs for increasing Chinese citizens' access to independent information, whether they are inside or outside China. The strategy must include eight main parts. First, a plan for making it easier for people in China to get and use tools that get around censorship and secure communication tools, including an assessment of technical challenges and gaps that might affect putting the strategy into action. Second, an assessment of the government's ability to create and distribute Chinese language content, including whether US Government produced Chinese content can be accessed inside or outside China, how much Chinese citizens engage with different types of content, using surveys and other data to understand what different groups of Chinese citizens are interested in and concerned about, and identifying where there are gaps or duplicated efforts. Third, a description of how the State Department plans to improve coordination within the department and across agencies in developing and sharing compelling Chinese language content while avoiding duplication, and in funding outside organizations to develop tools for getting around censorship and securely sharing content. Fourth, a description of how the department plans to better connect high quality content with the tools used to access and share it. Fifth, a description of how the department plans to build networks with known and new Chinese language content creators and social media influencers through programs like media and internet freedom initiatives based outside China. Sixth, an assessment of how effective current content generated by the department is when shared within China, including by US embassies and consulates in China, and how the department plans to improve this. Seventh, a plan for increasing digital engagement with Chinese citizens who are living or traveling outside China by giving them temporary access to an uncensored internet. Eighth, a description of any additional resources, including more funding or authorities, needed to achieve the strategy's goals. The strategy must be unclassified but can include a classified section.

sections.title: Section 7  Establishment of the Global News Service
sections.text: This section changes existing law to create a new organization called the Global News Service. The United States Agency for Global Media can give annual grants to establish this service, which will collect, translate, distribute, and make available content created by Voice of America, Radio Free Europe Radio Liberty, Radio Free Asia, Middle East Broadcasting Networks, Office of Cuba Broadcasting, or any organization funded by or partnering with the US Agency for Global Media. The Global News Service will focus on content about China and China's harmful activities around the world, working with Voice of America and Radio Free Asia's Chinese language news services. The service will offer content in both Chinese and English to make fact based, uncensored China related news available to news organizations, independent journalists, and online content creators worldwide. It will prioritize making this content available to media outlets in countries influenced by Chinese Communist Party controlled media. The Chinese language news service will target Chinese people living abroad, while the English language service will target foreign media outlets looking for China related stories. The Global News Service must follow specific rules about where its headquarters can be located, how it can use grant money, what contracts it can make, and how it will be overseen. The service will be subject to the same oversight as other grant recipients and can be audited by the Government Accountability Office and inspected by the Inspector General of the State Department.

sections.title: Section 8  Increasing Coordination and Resources for Censorship Circumvention, Secure Sharing, and Content Creation Initiatives
sections.text: The President must create a special group of representatives from the State Department, National Security Council staff, and other federal agencies as needed. The President will also appoint a coordinator to lead this group. The coordinator will organize the group's work, oversee creating and carrying out the strategy described in section six, and oversee State Department efforts in consultation with relevant officials. The group will develop and carry out the strategy and increase coordination within the State Department and between agencies for Chinese language content development and sharing, internet tools to get around censorship, and secure content sharing tools designed specifically for China's censorship system. The State Department will oversee developing compelling, timely, and relevant Chinese language content for various audiences in China and sharing this content through different tools and platforms inside and outside China. The department will work to increase funding for programs and open source software that create new tools for getting around internet censorship and securely sharing content that are specifically designed to avoid China's censorship system. The department will also increase funding for media freedom, investigative journalism, and content development by establishing and expanding networks of individual and independent journalists, media companies, and social media influencers who investigate and produce articles, reports, and other content about real time social, political, and economic events in China that Chinese citizens are interested in. The department will increase the amount of effective, high quality Chinese language content shared through Mission China's social media and other platforms, material that can be shared with Chinese citizens living outside China, and content focusing on quality of life issues in the United States that relate to issues in China like food safety, environmental sustainability, health care, economic security, jobs, investments, treatment of women and marginalized populations, and government transparency. The department will also increase and improve Chinese language content directed at Chinese citizens and work with outside organizations to regularly conduct surveys to understand what issues are important to Chinese citizens. The US Agency for Global Media and related organizations will work with independent content creators, citizen journalists, and media organizations to collect, share, and amplify the most impactful Chinese language content to Chinese citizens. Radio Free Asia will deliver independent, uncensored China specific news in local languages and increase coverage and digital Chinese language programming on political, economic, and social issues in China. Voice of America will increase content of interest to Chinese citizens and provide insights to the special group about content development strategies and data about how Chinese citizens access and engage with Voice of America Chinese language content. The Open Technology Fund will support developing open source tools for getting around censorship and secure communications designed for use in China, increase engagement with private technology companies and universities to develop next generation internet tools, issue public requests for students and civil society groups to research and develop new tools targeting China's censorship system, and regularly consult with the special group about circumvention tools and technical needs. The Global News Service will collect, translate, distribute, and make available content about China and China's harmful global activities, offer content in Chinese and English for fact based, uncensored China related news, prioritize making content available to media outlets in countries influenced by Chinese Communist Party state media, and target Chinese people living abroad through its Chinese language news service. For fiscal years two thousand twenty five through two thousand twenty nine, twenty five million dollars per year is authorized for the State Department and fifty million dollars per year is authorized for the US Agency for Global Media to carry out these functions.

sections.title: Section 9  Addressing the Lack of Reciprocity Between the United States and the People's Republic of China in the Information Space
sections.text: When engaging diplomatically with China, the Secretary of State should prioritize addressing the unfair situation where US Government, private sector, and nongovernmental stakeholders, especially journalists, diplomats, researchers, academics, internet technology and social media companies, and nongovernmental organizations, do not have equal access to China's internet and broader information space compared to what China has in the United States. The President, working with the Secretary of State, should consider all available tools to address this unfair treatment in access to China's internet and information space for US Government, private sector, and nongovernmental stakeholders.
